title: Trees & BSTs
description: Trees are in every FAANG interview. Beyond basic traversals, you must know LCA, serialize/deserialize, diameter, BST operations, and when to go iterative to avoid stack overflow.

sections.title: Tree Node & Traversal Templates
sections.description: Memorize the iterative versions — they avoid RecursionError on skewed trees (depth up to 10^5 in FAANG tests) and show interviewer you understand the call stack.

sections.title: Height, Diameter & Balance
sections.description: These three are computed via a single post-order DFS. The key insight: pass information up the tree from leaf to root, computing at each node based on children's results.
sections.codeBlock:
def height(root):
    if not root: return 0
    return 1 + max(height(root.left), height(root.right))

# Diameter — longest path between any two nodes (may not pass through root!)
def diameter(root):
    result = [0]
    def dfs(node):
        if not node: return 0
        left = dfs(node.left)
        right = dfs(node.right)
        result[0] = max(result[0], left + right)  # path through this node
        return 1 + max(left, right)               # height returned up
    dfs(root)
    return result[0]

# Check balanced — O(N) single pass
def is_balanced(root):
    def check(node):
        if not node: return 0
        left = check(node.left)
        right = check(node.right)
        if left == -1 or right == -1: return -1  # already unbalanced
        if abs(left - right) > 1: return -1      # this node unbalanced
        return 1 + max(left, right)
    return check(root) != -1

# Max path sum (can be negative weights)
def max_path_sum(root):
    best = [float('-inf')]
    def dfs(node):
        if not node: return 0
        left = max(dfs(node.left), 0)   # ignore negative contributions
        right = max(dfs(node.right), 0)
        best[0] = max(best[0], node.val + left + right)
        return node.val + max(left, right)
    dfs(root)
    return best[0]

sections.title: Lowest Common Ancestor (LCA)
sections.description: LCA is a classic FAANG problem. For a general binary tree (no BST property), use the recursive approach: if current node is p or q, return it; otherwise, combine left and right subtree results.

sections.title: Serialize & Deserialize Binary Tree
sections.description: A classic FAANG hard problem (LeetCode 297). Key insight: preorder traversal + null markers uniquely reconstructs any binary tree. BFS-based (level-order) is also common and produces the format used by LeetCode's visualizer.

sections.title: BST Operations & Validation
sections.description: BST property: left subtree all < node.val, right subtree all > node.val. Critical gotcha: Validation must check against a valid range — not just immediate children — otherwise a subtree with a valid local structure can violate the BST property globally.

sections.title: Staff+ — B-Trees & LSM-Trees
sections.description:
B-Tree: A self-balancing tree with branching factor B (typically 100–2000). Each node holds up to B−1 keys and B child pointers. The large branching factor keeps tree height to 3–4 levels (even for billions of keys), making it the standard index structure in every SQL database (InnoDB, PostgreSQL).

B+ Tree vs B-Tree: In a B+ tree, only leaf nodes store data. Internal nodes are routers (keys only). Leaves form a linked list for efficient range scans. This is the variant used by InnoDB, MongoDB WiredTiger, and SQLite.

LSM-Tree (Log-Structured Merge-Tree): Writes go to an in-memory memtable (sorted), flushed to immutable SSTable files. Reads check memtable → tier-0 SSTables → ... → tier-N. Background compaction merges SSTables. LSM-Trees separate write and read paths — writes are sequential (fast on HDD/SSD), reads need to merge multiple files. Used by LevelDB, RocksDB, Cassandra, Bigtable.

Staff+ decision framework: B-Tree for read-heavy OLTP (balanced read/write, strong consistency). LSM-Tree for write-heavy ingest (time-series, logs, event streams). Compaction is the biggest operational risk for LSM — monitor read amplification and compaction stalls.

sections.title: References & Further Reading
sections.description:
• deque for BFS — Use deque for O(1) BFS queue operations in tree traversal
  • Binary Search Trees in Python — BST implementation and common operations
  • binarytree — Python Library — Library for generating, visualizing, and manipulating binary trees
  • LeetCode — Tree Problems — Curated tree interview problems (DFS, BFS, LCA, Serialize, BST)
  • NeetCode — Tree Problems — Categorized FAANG tree problems with solutions
  • Cracking the Coding Interview (6th ed.) — Chapter 4: trees and graphs

details:
Trie (Prefix Tree): Each node represents a character prefix. Insert/Search/StartsWith all O(L) where L = word length. Used in autocomplete, spell-check, IP routing. Implement as dict of dicts or array[26] for lowercase letters. Mark end of word with a sentinel key.

Segment Tree: Answers range queries (sum, min, max) in O(log N) with O(log N) point updates. Build in O(N). Preferred over prefix sums when the array is mutable. For immutable arrays, prefix sums are simpler.

Red-Black / AVL Trees: Self-balancing BSTs that guarantee O(log N) all operations. Python's sortedcontainers.SortedList gives O(log N) insert/delete/rank queries — equivalent in practice. In interviews, mention 'I'd use a balanced BST / sortedcontainers' rather than implementing one from scratch.
